county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Trujillo Alto, established 1801, carved from Bayamón. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Trujillo Alto was carved from Bayamón in 1801 in the eastern San Juan metropolitan area. Parroquia Santa Cruz holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1801 records are in Bayamón's parish archives. As parent to Carolina (1857), pre-1857 Carolina records are here. The Carraízo reservoir and water treatment infrastructure displaced some communities — check relocation records. Suburban development from the 1950s transformed this formerly agricultural area — property records document the transition. St. Just area development created mid-century residential records. Check Bayamón church registers for pre-1801 families. Barrios like Carraízo, Cuevas, and La Gloria have distinct pre-suburban settlement histories.

source_profiles.source_name: Trujillo Alto Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Trujillo Alto municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1801.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Trujillo Alto Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Trujillo Alto / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Trujillo Alto including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Trujillo Alto Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Trujillo Alto district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1801. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Trujillo Alto Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Trujillo Alto. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Trujillo Alto Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Trujillo Alto district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Trujillo Alto district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Trujillo Alto Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Trujillo Alto / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Trujillo Alto. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Utuado. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Utuado, established 1739, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Utuado, established as a parish around 1739, is a major interior mountain municipio in the Cordillera Central. Parroquia San Miguel Arcángel holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent to Adjuntas (1815) and Jayuya (1911), Utuado records serve a wide central mountain area. The Caguana Indigenous Ceremonial Park (Taíno archaeological site) provides pre-colonial cultural context. Coffee cultivation dominated — Utuado was Puerto Rico's premier coffee region. Hurricane María (2017) devastated this mountain community and damaged some municipal records. The 1950 Nationalist uprising also affected Utuado — military and police records at NARA document the event. Notarial protocols at AGPR trace complex coffee hacienda family networks.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Vega Alta, established 1775, carved from Vega Baja. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Vega Alta was carved from Vega Baja in 1775 on the north coast. Parroquia Inmaculada Concepción holds records from the late 18th century. Pre-1775 records are in Vega Baja's parish archives. Sugar and pineapple cultivation generated agricultural records at AGPR. The coastal cerro (hill) area barrios and the interior valley barrios have distinct settlement patterns — check notarial protocols for each area separately. Vega Alta's proximity to both Dorado and Vega Baja means families frequently appear in multiple jurisdictions' records. Colonial-era cattle ranching preceded sugar — early land grants document this economic transition. Check civil registry from 1885 for vital records in the late Spanish period.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Vega Baja, established 1776, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Vega Baja, established as a parish around 1776, is a north-coast city. Known as 'La Ciudad del Melao Melao.' Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Rosario holds records from the late 18th century. As parent to Vega Alta (1775 — technically they separated simultaneously), the records are interrelated. Sugar cultivation dominated — Hacienda records at AGPR document both landowners and enslaved laborers. The melado (sugarcane syrup) tradition reflects the deep sugar heritage. Coastal fishing barrios (Cerro Gordo, Puerto Nuevo) have distinct maritime records. Barrios like Almirante Norte/Sur, Cabo Caribe, and Río Abajo have colonial-era settlement records in notarial protocols. Check colonial-era padrones for early population listings.

source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan

county_metadata.record_losses: US Navy occupation displaced communities and restricted access to parts of Vieques for decades. Some community records were lost during military land expropriations.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Vieques, established 1843, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Vieques, an island municipio southeast of Puerto Rico's main island, was established as a parish around 1843. Parroquia Inmaculada Concepción holds records from the 1840s. Earlier settlement records are sparse — Vieques had French, Danish, British, and Spanish competing claims. The US Navy used two-thirds of the island (1941-2003) — extensive military and civilian records at NARA document land expropriations, population displacement, and base operations. Enslaved persons records from sugar plantations (pre-1873) at AGPR are important. As parent to Culebra (1871), early Culebra records are here. English, French, and Creole-speaking Caribbean settlers make Vieques genealogy multilingual. Check British Virgin Islands and USVI records for family connections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Villalba, established 1917, carved from Juana Díaz, Coamo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Villalba was carved from Juana Díaz and Coamo in 1917 in the south-central mountains. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen holds records from 1917 — all earlier records are in Juana Díaz and Coamo parishes. As one of the later-formed municipios, civil registry and municipal records begin relatively late. The Toro Negro State Forest covers part of the municipio — check US Forest Service records at NARA. Coffee cultivation dominated the mountain economy — hacienda records at AGPR trace highland families. Cerro de Punta, Puerto Rico's highest peak (4,390 ft), is in Villalba. Check both Juana Díaz and Coamo's extensive parish archives for pre-1917 family records, as both are among PR's oldest settlements.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Yabucoa. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Yabucoa, established 1793, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yabucoa, established as a parish around 1793, is located in the southeastern corner of Puerto Rico in the Valle de Yabucoa. Parroquia Los Santos Ángeles Custodios holds records from the late 18th century. As parent to Maunabo (1799), early Maunabo records are here. Sugar plantations generated extensive enslaved persons records — the Yabucoa Valley was one of PR's most productive sugar regions. The Sun Oil Company refinery (later operated by others) brought 20th-century industrial workers. Hurricane María (2017) caused severe damage in Yabucoa, the first landfall area. Afro-Puerto Rican sugar worker families are well-documented in slave registers and church records. Check notarial protocols at AGPR for sugar estate transactions.

source_profiles.source_name: Yabucoa Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Yabucoa municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1793.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Yabucoa Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Yabucoa / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Yabucoa including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Yabucoa Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Yabucoa district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1793. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Yabucoa Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Yabucoa. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Yabucoa Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Yabucoa district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Yabucoa district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Yabucoa Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Yabucoa / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Yabucoa. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Yabucoa Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Yabucoa including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Yauco, established 1756, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yauco, established as a parish around 1756, is a major south-coast city known as 'La Ciudad del Café' for its premium coffee production. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Rosario holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent to Guánica (1914) and co-parent to Guayanilla, Yauco records serve a broad southwestern area. Corsican immigrants (particularly from the village of Luri) transformed Yauco's coffee industry — Corsican parish records in France complement local sources. The Yauco coffee aristocracy families left extensive notarial protocols at AGPR. Sugar plantations on the coastal plain generated enslaved persons records. The historic town center has been documented for its architectural heritage — property records trace building ownership.
